origin: A short excerpt from a biblical genealogy, listing a sequence of fathers in the lineage leading toward Jacob/Jesus in the opening chapter of the Gospel of Matthew (i.e., Matthew 1:15–16 region). The Greek wording suggests it comes from the New Testament tradition as transmitted in Greek manuscripts.
author: Most likely attributed to the Gospel writer Matthew (or more precisely, the author/collector of the Matthean Gospel), though the immediate wording could also reflect a later Greek manuscript tradition or a translator/copyist reproducing the text.
thoughts: The author’s perspective here is restrained and documentary: the purpose is to supply lineage credentials rather than narrative emotion. Implicitly, the author likely wants to emphasize continuity of fulfillment—showing that Jesus’s background is grounded in scriptural genealogical order, countering doubts by anchoring the story in recorded ancestry.
audience: Early Christian readers (especially those familiar with Jewish scripture/lineage traditions) who would understand the significance of genealogies and be interested in demonstrating messianic legitimacy and historical rootedness.
written_when: Likely in the late first century CE, commonly dated for Matthew to around ~80–90 CE (with some scholarly proposals earlier), based on typical dating of the Gospel composition and manuscript transmission traditions.
